# Area Chart

Area charts are line charts with the region below the line filled in, emphasizing the magnitude of trends over time. They work especially well for showing volume, cumulative totals, or comparing the relative size of multiple series.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

An area chart with default settings shows filled regions under each line with semi-transparent colors.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default area chart"
  designerSrc={area_chart_designer}
  designerAlt="Default area chart in designer mode"
  viewerSrc={area_chart_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default area chart in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### X-Axis Field (`xField`)

The time or category field for the horizontal axis.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={area_chart_props_designer}
  alt="X-axis field binding"
  caption="Configure x-axis field binding in the property panel"
/>

### Y-Axis Fields (`yFields`)

One or more measure fields. Each field produces a filled area series.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={area_chart_props_designer}
  alt="Multiple Y-axis fields"
  caption="Configure multiple y-axis fields in the property panel"
/>

### Smooth Curves (`smooth`)

When enabled, area boundaries are drawn as smooth curves.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={area_chart_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Smooth area curves"
  caption="Configure smooth area curves in the property panel"
/>

### Opacity (`opacity`)

Controls the transparency of the fill. Lower values let overlapping areas show through; higher values produce more vivid fills.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={area_chart_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Fill opacity adjustment"
  caption="Configure fill opacity adjustment in the property panel"
/>

### Stacked Areas (`stacked`)

When enabled, areas are stacked so each series starts where the previous one ends. This shows cumulative totals and the contribution of each series.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={area_chart_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Stacked areas"
  caption="Configure stacked areas in the property panel"
/>

## Tips

- Use **stacked areas** to show how components add up to a total over time
- Lower **opacity** when you have overlapping series to keep all areas visible
- For precise comparisons, a [Line Chart](/chart-types/line-chart/) may be clearer

## Related Features

- [Line Chart](/chart-types/line-chart/) — line without fill
- [Combo Chart](/chart-types/combo-chart/) — mix area with other series types

# Combo Chart

Combo charts combine multiple chart types (line, bar, area) in a single visualization, optionally with dual Y-axes. Use them when you need to compare measures with different scales or show different data perspectives together.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A combo chart with default settings shows a mix of bar and line series.

<ScreenshotComparison

Define multiple series, each with its own chart type (line, bar, or area), data field, and axis assignment.

- Use **dual axes** when measures have very different scales (e.g., revenue in millions vs. count in hundreds)

- The combo chart is the most flexible but also the easiest to over-complicate

# Chart Types

Supersubset includes 14 chart types powered by Apache ECharts. Each chart type is optimized for different kinds of data and use cases.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={galleryViewer}
  alt="Chart Gallery page showing all available chart types"
  caption="The Chart Gallery page demonstrates all 14 chart types with fixture data."
/>

## Standard Charts

| Chart | Best For | Data Shape |
|-------|----------|------------|
| [Line Chart](/chart-types/line-chart/) | Trends over time | Time series with 1+ measures |
| [Bar Chart](/chart-types/bar-chart/) | Comparing categories | Categorical with 1+ measures |
| [Area Chart](/chart-types/area-chart/) | Volume trends over time | Time series with stacking |
| [Pie Chart](/chart-types/pie-chart/) | Part-to-whole composition | Single measure by category |
| [Scatter Chart](/chart-types/scatter-chart/) | Correlation between variables | Two numeric dimensions |

## Advanced Charts

| Chart | Best For | Data Shape |
|-------|----------|------------|
| [Radar Chart](/chart-types/radar-chart/) | Multi-dimensional comparison | Multiple measures per entity |
| [Funnel Chart](/chart-types/funnel-chart/) | Conversion stages | Ordered categorical values |
| [Gauge](/chart-types/gauge/) | Single metric against target | Single value with min/max |
| [Heatmap](/chart-types/heatmap/) | Density across two dimensions | Grid of values |
| [Sankey Diagram](/chart-types/sankey/) | Flow between categories | Source → target with values |
| [Treemap](/chart-types/treemap/) | Hierarchical composition | Nested categories with values |
| [Waterfall Chart](/chart-types/waterfall/) | Running totals and deltas | Sequential additive values |
| [Box Plot](/chart-types/box-plot/) | Statistical distribution | Grouped numeric values |
| [Combo Chart](/chart-types/combo-chart/) | Mixed visualizations | Multiple series with different types |

## Common Configuration

All chart types share these base configuration options:

- **Title** — Display name shown above the chart
- **Dataset** — The data source to query
- **Field bindings** — Map your data fields to chart dimensions (X-axis, Y-axis, etc.)

Individual chart types add their own specific options, documented on each chart's page.

- Use **filled** mode when comparing two or three entities to see area overlap
- Ensure all dimensions use comparable scales — otherwise normalize your data

# Welcome to Supersubset

Supersubset is a visual dashboard builder that lets you create interactive analytics dashboards without writing code. You can drag and drop charts, tables, KPI cards, and other widgets onto a canvas, bind them to your data, configure filters and interactions, and publish a live dashboard for your users.

<ExpandAll />

## What You Can Build

With Supersubset, you can create dashboards that include:

- **14 chart types** — Line, Bar, Area, Pie, Scatter, Radar, Funnel, Gauge, Heatmap, Sankey, Treemap, Waterfall, Box Plot, and Combo charts
- **KPI cards** — Single-value metrics with trend comparisons
- **Data tables** — Sortable, paginated tabular views
- **Rich text** — Markdown-formatted content blocks
- **Alert widgets** — Operational alerts and notifications
- **Filters** — Global and scoped filters that update charts in real time
- **Cross-filtering** — Click a chart element to filter all related widgets
- **Multi-page dashboards** — Organize content across multiple pages with navigation
- **Import/Export** — Share dashboard definitions as JSON

## The Designer Interface

The Supersubset designer is a visual editor where you build your dashboards. It consists of:

1. **Widget Palette** (left) — Drag chart types, widgets, and layout components onto the canvas
2. **Canvas** (center) — The live preview of your dashboard layout
3. **Property Panel** (right) — Configure the selected widget's settings
4. **Toolbar** (top) — Undo/Redo, page management, code view, import/export

:::tip
Every feature in this documentation includes **before and after screenshots** showing both the designer configuration and the resulting viewer output. Click any screenshot to view it full-size.
:::

## Next Steps

- [Build your first dashboard →](/getting-started/first-dashboard/)
- [Explore chart types →](/chart-types/overview/)

- Users expect to click a bar/slice and see the rest of the dashboard update
- Combine with explicit [Select Filters](/filters/select-filter/) for a "Reset All" capability
- Keep the interaction intuitive — filter on the same field that labels the clicked element

# Filter Scope

Filter scope determines which widgets are affected when a viewer changes a filter value. By default, filters are **global** — they affect all widgets. You can narrow the scope to target specific widgets or pages.

<ExpandAll />

## Scope Types

### Global Scope

The default. A global filter affects every widget on every page of the dashboard.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Global filter scope"
  designerSrc={filter_builder_designer}
  designerAlt="Global filter scope in designer mode"
/>

### Widget-Specific Scope

Limit a filter to affect only specific widgets. Useful when different sections of a dashboard use different datasets.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={filter_bar_default_viewer}
  alt="Widget-specific scope"
  caption="Widget-specific scope"
/>

## Tips

- Start with **global scope** — it works for most dashboards
- Use **widget-specific scope** when you have widgets from different datasets on the same page
- Cross-filtering (click-based filtering) has its own scope rules — see [Cross-Filtering](/filters/cross-filtering/)

## Related Features

- [Select Filter](/filters/select-filter/) — dropdown filtering
- [Date Filter](/filters/date-filter/) — date range filtering
- [Cross-Filtering](/filters/cross-filtering/) — click-based filtering

# Click Actions

Click actions define what happens when a user clicks an element within a chart or widget. You can use click actions to filter other widgets, navigate to another page, or trigger custom behaviors.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Behavior

Without any click actions configured, clicking a chart element does nothing (or shows a tooltip).

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="No click action configured"
  designerSrc={interaction_editor_designer}
  designerAlt="No click action configured in designer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Filter Action

Clicking a chart element filters other widgets by the clicked value. This is [Cross-Filtering](/filters/cross-filtering/) functionality.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={click_actions_panel_designer}
  alt="Filter click action"
  caption="Filter click action"
/>

### Navigate Action

Clicking a chart element navigates to another dashboard page, optionally passing context.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={interaction_editor_default_designer}
  alt="Navigate click action"
  caption="Navigate click action"
/>

## Tips

- Use the [Interaction Editor](/interactions/drill-down/) to configure click actions visually
- Only configure click actions on charts where clicking a specific element is intuitive (bar charts, pie charts, table rows)
- Provide visual feedback so users know elements are clickable

## Related Features

- [Cross-Filtering](/filters/cross-filtering/) — the most common click action
- [Drill-Down](/interactions/drill-down/) — progressive detail exploration
- [Page Navigation](/pages/navigation/) — navigating between pages

# Select Filter

Select filters present a dropdown list of values from a data field, letting dashboard viewers narrow down data across all (or specific) widgets. The most common filter type for categorical data.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A select filter with default settings shows a dropdown with all unique values from the bound field.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default select filter"
  viewerSrc={select_filter_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default select filter in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Field Reference (`fieldRef`)

The data field whose unique values populate the dropdown options.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={filter_builder_default_designer}
  alt="Field reference binding"
  caption="Field reference binding"
/>

### Dataset Reference (`datasetRef`)

The dataset containing the field. This determines which data source provides the filter values.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={filter_builder_default_designer}
  alt="Dataset reference"
  caption="Dataset reference"
/>

### Operator (`operator`)

The comparison operator: `equals` (exact match), `contains`, `in` (multiple values).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={select_filter_active_viewer}
  alt="Filter operator options"
  caption="Filter operator options"
/>

### Scope

Controls which widgets are affected by this filter. See [Filter Scope](/filters/filter-scope/) for details.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={select_filter_active_viewer}
  alt="Filter scope configuration"
  caption="Filter scope configuration"
/>

## Tips

- Place filters in a **filter bar** at the top of the dashboard
- Use select filters for fields with **a limited set of values** (regions, categories, statuses)
- For date ranges, use a [Date Filter](/filters/date-filter/) instead
- Filters update all widgets in their scope in real time

## Related Features

- [Date Filter](/filters/date-filter/) — date range selection
- [Filter Scope](/filters/filter-scope/) — control which widgets a filter affects
- [Cross-Filtering](/filters/cross-filtering/) — click-based filtering

# Drill-Down

Drill-down interactions let users click a chart element to explore more detailed data. For example, clicking a region in a bar chart could show city-level data within that region.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Behavior

Without drill-down configured, charts show data at their configured aggregation level.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Chart without drill-down"
  designerSrc={interaction_editor_designer}
  designerAlt="Chart without drill-down in designer mode"
/>

## Configuration

Drill-down is configured through the Interaction Editor panel in the designer. Define:

1. **Source widget** — which chart supports drilling
2. **Trigger** — the click action
3. **Target behavior** — filter, navigate to a detail page, or update the chart's grouping

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={click_actions_panel_designer}
  alt="Drill-down configuration"
  caption="Drill-down configuration"
/>

## Tips

- Drill-down works best with **hierarchical data** (country → region → city)
- Provide a **breadcrumb or back button** so users can navigate back up
- Combine with [Page Navigation](/pages/navigation/) for deep drill-down flows across pages

## Related Features

- [Click Actions](/interactions/click-actions/) — general click behaviors
- [Cross-Filtering](/filters/cross-filtering/) — filter-based click actions
- [Multi-Page Dashboards](/pages/multi-page/) — navigate to detail pages

# Grid

The grid is the foundational layout container for every dashboard page. It uses a 12-column system where widgets and rows are placed within the grid. All other layout components (rows, columns, widgets) live inside a grid.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A grid with default settings provides a 12-column responsive container.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default grid layout"
  designerSrc={overview_designer}
  designerAlt="Default grid layout in designer mode"
  viewerSrc={overview_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default grid layout in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Columns (`columns`)

The number of columns in the grid (default: 12). Most dashboards use 12 columns for maximum flexibility.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={grid_overview_designer}
  alt="Grid column configuration"
  caption="Grid column configuration"
/>

## Tips

- Use the **12-column grid** to create layouts like: 4+4+4 (three equal), 8+4 (main + sidebar), 6+6 (half-half)
- Widgets specify their width in grid columns (e.g., `width: 6` fills half the grid)
- Rows within a grid group widgets on the same horizontal line

## Related Features

- [Rows & Columns](/layout/rows-columns/) — horizontal grouping within the grid
- [Tabs](/layout/tabs/) — tabbed content within the grid

# Rows & Columns

Rows group widgets on the same horizontal line. Within a row, each widget's `width` (in grid columns) determines how much horizontal space it takes. Columns can be used for vertical stacking within a row.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A row with widgets placed side by side, each occupying their configured width.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default row layout"
  designerSrc={overview_designer}
  designerAlt="Default row layout in designer mode"
  viewerSrc={overview_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default row layout in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Widget Width

Each widget within a row specifies its width in grid columns (1–12). Widgets that exceed the row's column count wrap to the next line.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={rows_columns_overview_viewer}
  alt="Widget width configuration"
  caption="Widget width configuration"
/>

### Widget Height

Each widget specifies its height in pixels. Charts typically use 300–400px; KPI cards use 100–150px.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={rows_columns_overview_viewer}
  alt="Widget height configuration"
  caption="Widget height configuration"
/>

## Tips

- Use **4+4+4** for three equal-width KPI cards
- Use **8+4** for a main chart with a sidebar widget
- Use **12** for full-width tables or headers
- Rows automatically handle responsive wrapping on smaller screens

## Related Features

- [Grid](/layout/grid/) — the parent container for rows
- [Tabs](/layout/tabs/) — alternative grouping as tabbed sections

# Tabs

Tabs let you organize content into switchable sections within a single dashboard page. Users click tab headers to switch between different views without navigating to a separate page.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A tabs component shows a row of tab headers with the active tab's content displayed below.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default tabs layout"
  viewerSrc={multi_page_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default tabs layout in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Tab Management

Add, rename, reorder, and delete tabs. Each tab contains its own set of widgets and layout components.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={tabs_viewer_tabs_viewer}
  alt="Tab management in designer"
  caption="Tab management in designer"
/>

## Tips

- Use tabs to **group related content** without creating separate pages
- Keep tab labels short (1–3 words)
- For major content sections, consider [Multi-Page Dashboards](/pages/multi-page/) instead
- Tabs work well for: different time periods, data segments, or metric categories

## Related Features

- [Multi-Page Dashboards](/pages/multi-page/) — full-page navigation
- [Grid](/layout/grid/) — layout within each tab

# Multi-Page Dashboards

Supersubset dashboards can contain multiple pages, each with its own layout and widgets. Pages appear as tabs or a navigation menu, letting you organize large dashboards into logical sections.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A new dashboard starts with a single page. The page management controls in the designer toolbar let you add more pages.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Single page dashboard"
  viewerSrc={multi_page_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Single page dashboard in viewer mode"
/>

## Managing Pages

### Adding a Page

Click the "Add Page" button in the toolbar. A new empty page is created with a default title.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={page_management_default_designer}
  alt="Adding a new page"
  caption="Adding a new page"
/>

### Renaming a Page

Double-click the page tab or use the page settings to change its title.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={page_management_default_designer}
  alt="Renaming a page"
  caption="Renaming a page"
/>

### Reordering Pages

Drag page tabs to reorder them in the navigation.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={multi_page_page1_viewer}
  alt="Reordering pages"
  caption="Reordering pages"
/>

### Deleting a Page

Remove a page from the page management controls. If you delete the active page, the dashboard switches to the nearest remaining page.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={multi_page_page1_viewer}
  alt="Deleting a page"
  caption="Deleting a page"
/>

## Tips

- Use pages for **distinct analytical perspectives** (Overview, Detail, Comparison)
- The Sales Dashboard example uses two pages: "Overview" and "Chart Gallery"
- Keep page count manageable — **3–5 pages** covers most dashboards
- Use [Click Actions](/interactions/click-actions/) to link from one page to another

## Related Features

- [Page Navigation](/pages/navigation/) — how users navigate between pages
- [Tabs](/layout/tabs/) — in-page content switching (lighter than full pages)

# Page Navigation

When a dashboard has multiple pages, users see navigation controls (tabs or a nav bar) that let them switch between pages. Navigation preserves filter state so users don't lose context when switching.

<ExpandAll />

## Navigation in the Viewer

The viewer presents page navigation as tab headers at the top of the dashboard.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Page navigation in viewer"
  viewerSrc={pages_scenario_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Page navigation in viewer in viewer mode"
/>

## Navigation in the Designer

The designer shows page tabs in the toolbar area. Click a tab to switch to that page for editing.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={pages_scenario_page1_viewer}
  alt="Page navigation in designer"
  caption="Page navigation in designer"
/>

## Tips

- **Filter state persists** across page navigation — users don't need to reapply filters
- Use clear, descriptive page titles so users know what to expect on each page
- Configure [Click Actions](/interactions/click-actions/) to programmatically navigate users to specific pages

# Alerts

The Alerts widget displays a list of operational alerts or notifications, color-coded by severity. Use it for monitoring dashboards, operations centers, or any scenario where users need to see a prioritized list of events.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

An alerts widget with default settings shows a list of alert cards with severity badges and timestamps.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default alerts widget"
  designerSrc={alerts_designer}
  designerAlt="Default alerts widget in designer mode"
  viewerSrc={alerts_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default alerts widget in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Title Field (`titleField`)

The field used for each alert's headline.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_designer}
  alt="Title field binding"
  caption="Configure title field binding in the property panel"
/>

### Message Field (`messageField`)

The field used for each alert's description text.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_designer}
  alt="Message field binding"
  caption="Configure message field binding in the property panel"
/>

### Severity Field (`severityField`)

The field that determines the color coding: `critical` (red), `warning` (amber), `info` (blue).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_designer}
  alt="Severity color coding"
  caption="Configure severity color coding in the property panel"
/>

### Timestamp Field (`timestampField`)

The datetime field shown as the alert's time.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_designer}
  alt="Timestamp display"
  caption="Configure timestamp display in the property panel"
/>

### Layout (`layout`)

Choose between `wrap` (cards flow horizontally and wrap) and `list` (vertical stack).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Layout: wrap vs list"
  caption="Configure layout: wrap vs list in the property panel"
/>

### Max Items (`maxItems`)

Limit the number of alerts displayed. Useful for showing only the most recent or critical items.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Max items limit"
  caption="Configure max items limit in the property panel"
/>

### Show Timestamp (`showTimestamp`)

Toggle timestamp visibility.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Timestamp visibility"
  caption="Configure timestamp visibility in the property panel"
/>

### Default Severity (`defaultSeverity`)

The severity used when the data doesn't include a severity field.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Default severity setting"
  caption="Configure default severity setting in the property panel"
/>

### Empty State (`emptyState`)

What to show when there are no alerts: `placeholder` (friendly message) or `hidden` (widget disappears).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={alerts_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Empty state behavior"
  caption="Configure empty state behavior in the property panel"
/>

## Tips

- Place at the **top of a dashboard** for visual priority
- Use **maxItems** to keep the widget compact — 3–5 items is usually enough
- Use **wrap layout** for a horizontal banner style; **list layout** for a vertical sidebar style
- Pair with [Filters](/filters/select-filter/) to let users filter by severity

## Related Features

- [KPI Card](/widgets/kpi-card/) — summary count of alerts
- [Table](/widgets/table/) — full alert history

# KPI Card

KPI cards display a single metric prominently, optionally with a comparison to a previous value showing trend direction. They're the most common widget for executive dashboards and summary rows.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A KPI card with default settings shows a large number with a title.

<ScreenshotComparison
  label="Default KPI card"
  designerSrc={kpi_card_designer}
  designerAlt="Default KPI card in designer mode"
  viewerSrc={kpi_card_viewer}
  viewerAlt="Default KPI card in viewer mode"
/>

## Configuration Options

### Value Field (`valueField`)

The numeric field to display as the primary value.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={kpi_card_props_designer}
  alt="Value field binding"
  caption="Configure value field binding in the property panel"
/>

### Comparison Field (`comparisonField`)

An optional field used to calculate and display the change from a previous period. Shows as an up/down arrow with percentage change.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={kpi_card_props_designer}
  alt="Comparison field showing trend"
  caption="Configure comparison field showing trend in the property panel"
/>

### Format (`format`)

Controls number formatting: `compact` (1.2K, 3.4M), `currency` ($1,234.56), or default (1234).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={kpi_card_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Format options comparison"
  caption="Configure format options comparison in the property panel"
/>

### Prefix (`prefix`)

A text prefix added before the value (e.g., `$`, `€`).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={kpi_card_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Currency prefix"
  caption="Configure currency prefix in the property panel"
/>

### Suffix (`suffix`)

A text suffix added after the value (e.g., `%`, `units`).

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={kpi_card_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Unit suffix"
  caption="Configure unit suffix in the property panel"
/>

## Tips

- Group **3–4 KPI cards** in a row at the top of your dashboard for an executive summary
- Use **comparison field** to show month-over-month or year-over-year trends
- Use **compact format** for large numbers to avoid visual clutter
- For target-based metrics, consider a [Gauge](/chart-types/gauge/) instead

## Related Features

- [Gauge](/chart-types/gauge/) — metric with a visual range
- [Table](/widgets/table/) — detailed data behind the KPI

# Table

Tables display data in a familiar rows-and-columns format with support for pagination, sorting, and column configuration. Use tables for detail views where users need to see individual records.

<ExpandAll />

## Default Appearance

A table with default settings shows all available fields with automatic column widths and pagination.

## Configuration Options

### Page Size (`pageSize`)

The number of rows displayed per page. Smaller pages load faster; larger pages show more data at once.

<FeatureScreenshot
  src={table_props_scrolled_designer}
  alt="Page size adjustment"
  caption="Configure page size adjustment in the property panel"
/>

## Tips

- Place tables at the **bottom of a dashboard** as a detail/drill-down view
- Use [Filters](/filters/select-filter/) to let users narrow down table data
- For a single summary number, use a [KPI Card](/widgets/kpi-card/) instead

## Related Features

- [KPI Card](/widgets/kpi-card/) — summary metrics from table data
